channel.title: NYT > Business
channel.copyright: Copyright 2026 The New York Times Company

item.title: Dang Van Phuoc Dies at 90; Intrepid Photojournalist in Vietnam War
item.description: A photographer for The Associated Press, he spent a decade on the front lines of combat in his native Vietnam and lost his right eye in a grenade explosion.
item.dc:creator: Richard Sandomir

item.title: When War Breaks Out Over the Family Business, They Get the Call
item.description: As baby boomers begin passing down trillions in assets, advisers who specialize in navigating the process — and the emotions around it — have never been busier.
item.dc:creator: Brent Crane
item.media:credit: Sophie Park for The New York Times
item.media:description: Doug Baumoel founded Continuity Family Business Consulting, in Massachusetts, to “help families prevent, manage and heal from conflict over business and money.”

item.title: How Your Friend Group Influences How You Spend and Save
item.description: Our spending habits are often influenced by our social circles. You can help shape what it looks like.
item.dc:creator: Paulette Perhach
item.media:credit: Benjamin Norman for The New York Times
item.media:description: Sandy Smith said she embraced being “cheap” because “I had to.” Now her friends come to her for financial advice.

item.title: Making Motherhood and a Long Tennis Career Possible
item.description: Tennis is a solitary sport, but with new fertility benefits, a sisterhood of egg freezers is emerging in locker rooms and players’ lounges.
item.dc:creator: Tania Ganguli
item.media:credit: Matthew Avignone for The New York Times
item.media:description: “I have found this absolutely newfound admiration for women that go through those cycles over and over,” Magda Linette said.

item.title: China’s Rise in Drug Development Looms Over U.S.
item.description: Clinical trials in China are getting attention at an international oncology gathering in Chicago. China’s surging biotechnology industry is fueling alarm that U.S. dominance in the field is waning.
item.dc:creator: Rebecca Robbins and Gina Kolata
item.media:description: The American Society of Clinical Oncology annual meeting in Chicago last year. One of the conference’s coveted headliners this year will be a presentation of a clinical trial conducted only in China.

item.title: S.E.C. Proposes to Kill Climate Change Disclosure Rule
item.description: The regulation would have required all publicly traded companies to disclose whether they faced significant risks from climate change and its effects.
item.dc:creator: Maxine Joselow
item.media:credit: Loren Elliott for The New York Times
item.media:description: An oil drilling site in Wyoming. The S.E.C. rule would have required companies to disclose their contributions to climate change and the risks they faced from a warming planet.

item.title: For Wall Street, the Only Thing Worse Than SpaceX Flopping Is Missing Out
item.description: Elon Musk and his bankers are working to create a self-fulfilling prophecy for the massive public offering: Make investors feel that the risks of passing it up outweigh those of buying into it.
item.dc:creator: Maureen Farrell and Lauren Hirsch
item.media:credit: Meridith Kohut for The New York Times
item.media:description: SpaceX’s launch site near Boca Chica, Texas, in 2024. The company could go public as soon as next month.

item.title: Sky-High I.P.O. Pricing Isn’t Great for Real People
item.description: When newly public companies have been valued as richly as SpaceX, OpenAI and Anthropic seem likely to be, the outlook for ordinary investors has been poor.
item.dc:creator: Jeff Sommer
item.media:description: SpaceX’s Starship rocket during a test flight in May. The company’s chief executive, Elon Musk, stands to unlock a great fortune when the company goes public.

item.title: Inside the Room Where War Insurance Is Bought and Sold
item.description: Many of the ships stranded in the Persian Gulf depend on coverage negotiated at Lloyd’s, the center of marine insurance for more than 300 years.
item.dc:creator: Jenny Gross
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Joseph Horton for The New York Times
item.media:description: The 200-foot-tall atrium in the Underwriting Room, where brokers negotiate policies for ships carrying oil, gas and other goods around the world.

item.title: Trump Clears Way for Companies to Avoid Taxes in Havens Including Malta and Cyprus
item.description: U.S. companies skirted at least $40 billion in taxes since the beginning of 2025 thanks to schemes in places like Malta, Bermuda and Cyprus.
item.dc:creator: Jesse Drucker and Dylan Freedman
item.media:credit: Susan Wright for The New York Times
item.media:description: A favorite destination for tax-dodging by U.S. companies was the tiny Mediterranean island of Malta.

item.title: The $900 Billion Giant: How Anthropic Got So Big, So Fast
item.description: The artificial intelligence giant was just valued at $900 billion, surpassing OpenAI. Here are the numbers behind its rise — and headwinds it faces.
item.dc:creator: Andrew Ross Sorkin, Bernhard Warner, Sarah Kessler, Michael J. de la Merced, Niko Gallogly, Brian O’Keefe, Ian Mount and Lauren Hirsch
item.media:credit: Jason Henry for The New York Times
item.media:description: Anthropic, which created the Claude A.I. model, has surged in popularity with users, pushing its valuation to $900 billion.

item.title: Luka Doncic on Trying to Bring an N.B.A. Team to Rome
item.description: The superstar is part of group that has bought a basketball team in Italy, with a plan to move the club to Rome in hopes of making it part of a new league.
item.dc:creator: Tania Ganguli
item.media:description: “Since I came to the N.B.A., my dream was always to own a team in Europe,” said Luka Doncic, a Slovenian basketball star who plays for the Los Angeles Lakers.

item.title: Providence Isn’t an Official World Cup Host but It Sure Is Acting Like One
item.description: A stadium outside Boston that will host seven World Cup games is actually closer to Providence, R.I., prompting officials there to capitalize on that proximity to lure fans.
item.dc:creator: Adeel Hassan
item.media:credit: Kieran Dodds for The New York Times
item.media:description: Fans of the Scottish soccer team in Glasgow in 2021. Fans of Scotland’s World Cup team are expected to fill accommodations in Providence, R.I., to watch matches at a Massachusetts stadium next month.

item.title: A.I. Doesn’t Have to Mean Layoffs
item.description: A French multinational, Schneider Electric, decided to use artificial intelligence in manufacturing to make workers more productive, rather than to replace them. Here’s how that’s going.
item.dc:creator: Patricia Cohen and Alexis Pazoumian

item.title: ‘Backrooms’ Is Part of a Boomlet in Movies From YouTube Creators
item.description: “Backrooms,” a psychological horror flick opening this weekend, is part of a wave of breakout films from fledgling directors who honed their instincts on YouTube.
item.dc:creator: Brooks Barnes
item.media:description: The director of “Backrooms,” Kane Parsons, left, on a set with one of the new film’s stars, Chiwetel Ejiofor.

item.title: How NextEra Energy Wielded Political Power in Florida
item.description: NextEra, which is seeking to buy Dominion Energy, has often butted heads with consumer groups, residents and journalists in its home state.
item.dc:creator: Ivan Penn
item.category: White House East Wing and Ballroom (Washington, DC)
item.media:description: A Dominion Energy power plant near Davis, W.Va. NextEra Energy’s political activities are expected to be scrutinized as it seeks approval of its purchase of Dominion.

item.title: How to Travel if You Have Panic Attacks
item.description: You don’t have to let severe anxiety keep you from exploring the world. Here’s how to manage your panic on vacation.
item.dc:creator: Ashley Lauretta

item.title: Oil Prices Fall as U.S. and Iran Edge Closer to Agreement
item.description: Oil prices and stock markets fluctuated as investors assessed tentative signs of a pact to extend a cease-fire.
item.dc:creator: The New York Times
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)

item.title: Europe Is Edging Closer to a Trade War With China. Here’s Why.
item.description: As cheap goods pour in, threatening the continent’s manufacturing sector, a search for solutions is becoming increasingly urgent.
item.dc:creator: Jeanna Smialek and Alexandra Stevenson
item.media:description: Chinese-made electric vehicles awaiting export at the port in Hangzhou, eastern China, in May.

item.title: ABC Accuses Trump Administration of Threatening Its Broadcast Licenses
item.description: The TV network slammed the Federal Communications Commission, saying the regulator was targeting its station licenses in a campaign of “unconstitutional retaliation.”
item.dc:creator: Jim Rutenberg
item.media:description: The filing was the second time this month that ABC accused the Federal Communications Commission of violating its free speech rights because of what the network has called “speech the government does not like.”

item.title: Treasury Prepares to Make Trump the Face of a New $250 Bill
item.description: Treasury Secretary Bessent backed the idea, which would require legislation allowing a living person to appear on currency.
item.dc:creator: Alan Rappeport
item.media:description: Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent said he supported the idea of putting President Trump’s portrait on a new $250 bill.

item.title: Two Papers Named ‘Star’ Will Duel in D.C. to Win Readers From The Washington Post
item.description: First, The Star was born. Now, The Washington Star is reborn. Both are hoping to win readers ceded by recent layoffs at The Washington Post.
item.dc:creator: Katie Robertson
item.media:description: The Washington Star, previously known as The Washington Star-News, stopped publishing in 1981.

item.title: Watchdog Agency Seeks to Reverse Its Win Against Firm Run by Winklevoss Twins
item.description: The Commodity Futures Trading Commission filed jointly with a company run by the Winklevoss twins to overturn their settlement.
item.dc:creator: Sharon LaFraniere
item.media:description: Gemini’s founders, Tyler and Cameron Winklevoss, are among the president’s closest allies in the crypto industry.

item.title: Everlane Co-Founder, Shocked by Shein Acquisition, Wants a Redo
item.description: Michael Preysman, who ran the brand for a decade, can’t believe what happened to the company he built. Can he start over?
item.dc:creator: Kim Bhasin
item.media:description: Michael Preysman, a co-founder of Everlane, is among a cohort of founders who have bemoaned the fate of their brands after giving up control.

item.title: Trump Refiles $10 Billion Lawsuit Against The Wall Street Journal
item.description: An earlier version of the defamation suit, which focused on an article about a birthday note to Jeffrey Epstein, was dismissed by a judge.
item.dc:creator: Katie Robertson
item.media:credit: Juan Arredondo for The New York Times
item.media:description: President Trump’s lawsuit centered on a Wall Street Journal story published in July 2025.

item.title: Bari Weiss Names Nick Bilton to Lead CBS’s ‘60 Minutes’ in Major Shake-Up
item.description: Bari Weiss, CBS’s editor in chief, named Nick Bilton, a tech journalist and filmmaker, as the show’s executive producer. The network also fired two on-air correspondents.
item.dc:creator: Benjamin Mullin and Michael M. Grynbaum
item.media:description: Nick Bilton is a filmmaker and former New York Times columnist.

item.title: Fired BP Chair Manifold Rejects ‘Lies’ About His Ouster
item.description: Albert Manifold, who was abruptly dismissed by the oil company this week, hit back in a new statement that admitted he “pushed hard” but never crossed a line.
item.dc:creator: Gregory Schmidt
item.media:description: Albert Manifold in 2012. During his relatively short tenure at BP, “it felt to me that my priorities were not always shared by everyone,” he said in a statement on Thursday.

item.title: Iran War Lifts a Second Inflation Reading to Highest Level Since 2023
item.description: Officials at the central bank have embraced the possibility of higher interest rates to get resurgent inflation under control.
item.dc:creator: Colby Smith
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)

item.title: The Google Insider Trading Case Hits Polymarket
item.description: Charges against a Google employee who bet on Polymarket have raised concerns that the issue may dent momentum in the fast-growing sector.
item.dc:creator: Andrew Ross Sorkin, Bernhard Warner, Sarah Kessler, Michael J. de la Merced, Niko Gallogly, Brian O’Keefe and Ian Mount
item.media:description: A new insider-trading case has rocked Polymarket, the popular prediction market platform.

item.title: Temu Hit With Fine in E.U. Over Sales of Unsafe Goods
item.description: The Chinese e-commerce platform faces a penalty of more than $230 million for selling baby toys and other products the European Commission said could harm consumers.
item.dc:creator: Alexandra Stevenson and Jeanna Smialek
item.media:credit: Ksenia Kuleshova for The New York Times
item.media:description: The action against Temu comes amid broader European scrutiny of Chinese companies that sell goods in the region. The European Commission is also investigating Shein and AliExpress, two of Temu’s Chinese rivals.

item.title: Genetic Scores are Booming. But Will Anti-Discrimination Laws Cover Your DNA?
item.description: As predictive medicine advances, legal scholars warn that decades-old federal guidelines could set up a potential clash between your genes and your job.
item.dc:creator: Emily Baumgaertner Nunn
item.media:description: A scanning electron micrograph of human chromosomes. Polygenic risk scoring, increasingly popular in personal medicine, uses an individual’s genome to estimate that person’s likelihood of developing a specific disease.

item.title: These Drivers Found Cheap Gas
item.description: It’s in Valley Center, Calif., on tribal land. Customers relish every penny saved.
item.dc:creator: Corina Knoll
item.media:credit: John Francis Peters for The New York Times
item.media:description: David Dickerson was among those who stopped at Horizon Fuel Center recently when its gas was going for $5.39 a gallon, well below the state average.

item.title: My $7,300 United Business Class Seat Was Broken, and I Got a Pittance
item.description: A woman splurged for a lie-flat seat, but it got jammed in the horizontal position for most of the 14-hour flight. How much should United compensate her?
item.dc:creator: Seth Kugel

item.title: Streaming TV Bundles Grow in Popularity
item.description: Companies and their customers are embracing bundled streaming packages — but for different reasons.
item.dc:creator: John Koblin

item.title: Australia Sues 3M for $1.4 Billion Over ‘Forever Chemicals’
item.description: The lawsuit, the largest ever filed by the Australian government, claims 3M hid information about the harmful effects of PFAS used at military bases.
item.dc:creator: Catie Edmondson and Laura Chung
item.media:description: 3M’s building in a suburb of Sydney, Australia, on Thursday.

item.title: Oil Prices Ease on Possible Agreement for Strait’s Reopening
item.description: Oil prices jumped after the U.S. said it shot down four Iranian attack drones, casting doubt once again on the prospects for a peace deal.
item.dc:creator: The New York Times
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Jonas Roosens/Belga, via Agence France-Presse — Getty Images

item.title: What Plunging Pork Prices Say About China’s Economy
item.description: A key measure of inflation in China, they hit a 16-year low, driven by anemic consumer spending and an oversupply of hogs.
item.dc:creator: Catie Edmondson
item.media:description: A pig farm in Taizhou, China. “Many hog farmers can no longer hold out,” Sun Haoyu, who raises hogs in Dalian, China.

item.title: Court Orders Customs Chief to Address Compliance on Refunding Tariffs
item.description: The surprising demand to appear at a hearing suggested new concern about the Trump administration’s efforts to repay the full $166 billion owed from illegally imposed tariffs.
item.dc:creator: Tony Romm
item.media:description: Rodney S. Scott, the head of U.S. Customs and Border Protection, has been ordered to appear before the Court of International Trade next month.

item.title: Condé Nast Pays Over $400,000 to 3 Journalists Fired Over Protest
item.description: The former workers were among a group of employees who confronted the company’s head of human resources about layoffs last fall.
item.dc:creator: Katie Robertson
item.media:credit: Victor Llorente for The New York Times
item.media:description: The Manhattan headquarters of Condé Nast, which reached a settlement with the NewsGuild of New York, the union representing workers the company fired.

item.title: KFF’s Chief Executive to Retire
item.description: Drew Altman, who transformed KFF from a little-known family foundation to a major source of U.S. health policy research, will step down at the end of the year.
item.dc:creator: Reed Abelson
item.media:description: Drew Altman speaking to the KFF board in 2019. He has been with the organization, a public charity, since 1990.

item.title: ’60 Minutes’ Journalist Sharyn Alfonsi Loses Deal After Dispute With Bari Weiss
item.description: Sharyn Alfonsi, whose segment on a brutal Salvadoran prison was pulled abruptly in December, said that CBS News and its top editor, Bari Weiss, had let her contract expire.
item.dc:creator: Michael M. Grynbaum
item.media:credit: Marla Aufmuth/Getty Images for Texas Conference for Women
item.media:description: Sharyn Alfonsi, a CBS News journalist, said the expiration of her contract sent “a chilling message to the entire newsroom.”

item.title: Ferrari’s First Electric Car Runs Into Backlash in Italy and Beyond
item.description: Memes mocked the new model, analysts questioned its appeal and investors sold the automaker’s stock. A former Ferrari chairman warned of “the destruction of a legend.”
item.dc:creator: Bernhard Warner
item.media:credit: Ferrari Press Office, via AFP -- Getty Images
item.media:description: For help designing the fully electric car, Ferrari turned to the agency founded by Jony Ive, Apple’s former design chief, and the industrial designer Marc Newson.

item.title: Global Supply Shortages Deepen as War Drags On, Risking Jobs and Growth
item.description: After three months, the fallout of the closure of the Strait of Hormuz is spreading, with developing countries bearing the brunt of the shortfall.
item.dc:creator: River Akira Davis, Catie Edmondson, Eshe Nelson, Peter S. Goodman and Suhasini Raj
item.category: Far East, South and Southeast Asia and Pacific Areas
US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: A liquefied natural gas production facility in Qatar before the war with Iran.

item.title: BP’s Former Chairman, Albert Manifold, Disputes How Company Characterized His Dismissal
item.description: Albert Manifold, who was abruptly fired less than a year after he was appointed, said he was let go “without explanation.”
item.dc:creator: Gregory Schmidt

item.title: Teachers Union Urges Schools to Curb A.I. Chatbots and Screen Time
item.description: The American Federation of Teachers recommended “no screens” at all for those in second grade or younger, and no A.I. chatbots for students in elementary school.
item.dc:creator: Natasha Singer
item.media:description: Randi Weingarten, president of the American Federation of Teachers, said on Wednesday that teaching and learning in the earliest grades “should be done without A.I.”

item.title: Why Memory Chips Are Dominating the A.I. Rally
item.description: Three major producers — Micron, Samsung and SK Hynix — are now trillion-dollar companies. Politicians and Wall Street have taken notice.
item.dc:creator: Andrew Ross Sorkin, Bernhard Warner, Sarah Kessler, Michael J. de la Merced, Niko Gallogly, Brian O’Keefe and Lauren Hirsch
item.media:credit: Liam Kennedy for The New York Times
item.media:description: Micron, the booming U.S. chipmaker, has become a darling of Wall Street and the Trump administration.

item.title: Samsung Unions Approve Pay Deal That Highlights Inequality of A.I. Age
item.description: The agreement all but guarantees hefty bonuses for employees in the top-performing chip unit. Other workers say they feel left out.
item.dc:creator: Max Kim
item.media:description: Choi Seung-ho, right, head of the Samsung Electronics Labor Union, celebrated with a Samsung executive after signing a tentative agreement earlier this month in Suwon, a city south of Seoul.

item.title: Spencer Pratt Wants to Be Mayor. His Fans Want Him to Be Batman.
item.description: What an A.I.-generated video of the former reality TV star teaches us about the future of political ads.
item.dc:creator: Willy Staley

item.title: Crypto Firms and Automakers Are Looking to Open Banks, at Trump’s Urging
item.description: Urged on by the White House and a reduction in regulatory oversight, crypto companies and automakers are among those that have applied for banking charters.
item.dc:creator: Stacy Cowley
item.category: Dodd-Frank Wall Street Reform and Consumer Protection Act (2010)
item.media:credit: Kim Raff for The New York Times
item.media:description: Paul Gu, the chief executive of Upstart, an online lender seeking a bank charter. “It was just a natural next step for us,” Mr. Gu said.
